data.title: P20260114 - PayPal - Lasting Cash Cow Model Disconnected From It's Actual Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

PayPal's business quality varies significantly by segment, and honest assessment requires acknowledging where defensibility is strong versus under pressure. The branded checkout and consumer wallet (PayPal button, peer-to-peer) remain structurally defensible through network effects and consumer trust180 million active accounts create switching costs and merchant acceptance. Unbranded processing (Braintree) is increasingly commodity-like with compressed margins, but generates massive volume that feeds higher-margin services. The fastest-growing segmentsbuy-now-pay-later, merchant services, value-added servicescommand premium economics and are where PayPal's future profitability concentrates.

The risk is that core payments become utility infrastructure while adjacent services face intense competition from Block, Affirm, and banking incumbents. I'm willing to concede unbranded checkout offers limited pricing power, but the bundled nature of PayPal's ecosystemwhere merchant cash advance customers also use fraud tools and BNPLcreates stickiness that pure-play competitors struggle to replicate.

Stock Chart 3-Year Performance

The 3-year comparisons shows a clear outperformance for FINX as it grew 76.53% in 3 years compared to the negative growth of 12.36% for PYPL. The chart is to a degree flawed as it was valued at a very high level during the years before that and in 2022. PYPLs 5-year Monthly beta is also 1.42 making it a very volatile asset to own. It's still lower than FINX which has a beta of 1.62 instead. This begs the question why the company has such a poor stock chart. I'll dive into this more just below here.

Overstated Pessimism - Now Just Plainly Unfounded

Since the bubble burst in 2021 along with many other high-growth tech stocks the market has done nothing other than award PayPal with a low valuation multiple, expecting little to no growth and in general a great deal of pessimism. That the stock was overvalued then is definitely a valid argument, but now? Not so much.

Paypal has undergone a shift in management with the introduction in 2023 of the new CEO, Alex Chriss. Since then, the company has continued to improve on margins and cut down on operating expenses. Since 2021, operating income has improved greatly, going from $3.22 billion in Q3 2020 to $6.05 billion in Q2 2025. When Alex joined the company as CEO it was in a decline, with consecutive quarters of declining operating income.

To still see such a low multiple applied seems to neglect all of the previous work and changes PayPal has undergone as a company.

Investments into AI solutions will likely lead to further cost improvements and margin expansions. Paypal Ventures, which is the part of the company investing in AI has made since its inception crucial capital investments in the likes of Rasa, Bureau and Finmo. These startups cover areas like fraud prevention to corporate treasury solutions. I will monitor carefully how these investments generate higher margins across the business. These assets are found under strategic investments as explained in the latest SEC filing from PayPal:

Our strategic investments include marketable equity securities, which are publicly traded, and non-marketable equity securities, which are primarily investments in privately held companies.

PayPal incurred a net gain of $59 million on strategic investments during the first months of 2025, marking a change from 2024 when it recorded a $55 million loss instead. Strategic investments are found under the long-term investments, valued currently at $1.576 billion, down from $1.559 billion on December 31, 2024. This points to no meaningful investments made during 2025 so far for PayPal, but given the $6.6 billion cash position, I expect the company to make meaningful investments in the coming years.

A Return To Normal - Valuation And Industry Hype Consolidate

At its height, PayPal enjoyed a 90x earnings multiple. Once the tech bubble burst, it dropped down to a 14/15x earnings multiple. This marks a return to normal for the company and the market in general. I say that as Palantir still trades at over 200x earnings or whatever absurd number it happens to be today. For a business like PayPal this return to normal I believe, is a good thing. I was not an investor in 2021 as I found the multiple way too high. At this level, it is much more attractive as an investment.

Between Q3 2020 and Q2 2025 net income has grown from $3.14 billion to $4.68 billion, translating to 49% growth. FCF margin has grown during the same measuring period from 24.9% to 31.3%.

The stock is currently up 25% from the 52-week lows, but still down 24,6% from the 52-week high of $93 per share. Investors get to buy the stock at a fair valuation, even slightly discounted if you believe PayPal can grow at an accelerated rate compared to market forecasts, pinning PayPal at 5.83% annual revenue growth the next three years.

P/E is currently 50% below the 5-year average for PayPal. The capital leverage which PayPal has is higher than a few years ago, but due to higher EBITDA, it remains manageable. For reference, LT debt is $11.2 billion and TTM EBITDA is $6.5 billion.

Since Alex took over as CEO, ROIC has risen sharply, sitting at 11.46% last quarter. When he took over in 2023 it was at 8.97%. These figures further support my argument that the management shift PayPal underwent is not reflected in the stock price, which is why I am writing this bullish thesis.

TPV Growth Through A Slower, But Steadier Plan

Which direction the share price swings after an earnings release often depends on what the TPV results are, at least from what I've observed the past 2 years. In the second quarter they grew 5%, slightly ahead of 4% in the first quarter.

Under the old CEO (Schulman), PayPal focused much more on aggressive growth, damned be margins and that stuff. This leads to a lot of growth but without a lot of substance unfortunately. Now with a new management style in place the plan is to go more steadily ahead and focus on creating reliable income streams. This is clearly seen as TPV growth has decelerated from a year ago. But, importantly, and I mean important, is that the operating margins have improved. The second quarter they grew 140 bps, first quarter 260 bps (on a yearly basis). This is what I think investors should focus on now. Not that some segment grew in the high teens. I pride myself in investing in value stocks, and these are the exact changes I want to see now for PayPal.

Unbranded checkouts is where much of the growth lies for PYPL now. Offering payment options in apps like Uber or Taco Bell it gathers carve out its own personal space. The launch of a Venmo debit card has also been highly successful growing 40% YoY with monthly active users. The contactless payments options like Pay with Venmo have proven another good investment. I know I used this all the time. I honestly can't remember the last time I pressed in my card code.

The Commoditization Critique: Where PayPal Still Defends Economics

The bear case isn't that PayPal is failing operationallyit's that payments are commoditizing, competitive pressure limits pricing power, and multiples may remain structurally depressed as a result. This deserves a direct response.

PayPal's defensibility sits in three layers. First, the unbranded checkout business (Braintree) is indeed becoming utility-like with compressed margins, but it generates $20+ billion in TPV and serves as customer acquisition for higher-margin services. Second, the branded checkout (PayPal button) retains pricing power because it bundles buyer and seller protections, fraud prevention, and working capital solutions that pure payment rails don't provide. Merchants pay 2.9% + $0.30 not just for transaction processing, but for conversion liftPayPal users complete purchases at 80%+ rates vs 60-70% for guest checkout.

Third, and most defensible, are the value-added services: buy-now-pay-later generating 25%+ net transaction margins, merchant cash advances, and fraud/risk management tools. These businesses are growing faster than core payments and command premium pricing because they solve distinct problems beyond moving money.

The risk is real: if PayPal becomes just another payment rail, multiples stay compressed. But the $2.6 billion in annual "other value added services" revenue growing at 10%+ suggests the company is successfully layering margin-accretive services onto the commodity rails. The question is whether this services mix shift happens fast enough to offset payment commoditizationI believe it does, bears disagree.

Buybacks Reward Long-Term Shareholders

What seems to go under the radar is the large buybacks PayPal continues to make. The past 5 years alone, shares have gone down by 18.4%, or 4.08% annually. During 2025 it has accelerated, going down 7.7% the first 6 months compared to 2024. In June 2022 the management approved a $15 billion buyback plan, which was extended in February 2025 when an additional $15 billion was approved to allocate for this. Last quarter, PayPal bought back shares for roughly $1.4 billion at an average price of $67, netting a 2.7% gain as of today's share price. 2025 guidance for share repurchasing remained the same following the latest quarter, standing strong at $6 billion in total. Given the market capitalization of $66 billion, there is another 4-5% in share reduction likely to occur in the last 6 months of the year.

The sustainability question deserves attention: can PayPal maintain $6 billion annual buybacks indefinitely? Free cash flow over the past 12 months was $6.8 billion, suggesting the current pace is fully covered by operating cash generation. However, $880 million of that FCF was consumed by working capital investments in the loan origination business. Adjusting for this, core FCF runs closer to $7.7 billion, providing comfortable coverage even if loan growth continues.

The risk is if operating margins compress or loan losses spikeeither scenario would pressure FCF and force buyback normalization to $4-5 billion annually. At that reduced pace, share count reduction slows from 7% to 4-5% annually, lowering EPS growth but not eliminating it. My base case assumes $5-6 billion sustained buybacks through 2029, not $6+ billiona conservative posture given management's $15 billion authorization and strong FCF generation.

Scalability - PayPal Can Still Muster Plenty More Growth

The scalability assessment is anchored in the market report that projects the Payment Processing Solutions sector will reach a $139 billion valuation in 2030. Although one report credits the company with a near 60% market share here, I find the perpetual continuation of that share to be overly optimistic. I have therefore based my calculations on more conservative estimates, ranging between 40% and 50% instead. The net margins used are founded on historical figures combined with growth forecasts.

The numbers shown in the cells represent the net income that would supposedly be generated. As previously established, PYPL has a strong history of executing share buybacks. My assumption includes a reduction in outstanding shares by 10% to 15% by 2030, or 877 million using the mid-range figure. This leads to an EPS range of $3.83 to $6.7, and a P/E range of 17.8 to 10.2. I personally do not believe the company will see its market share drop from 55% to 40% in just five years. My assumption is closer to a 48% to 50% range, coupled with increasing margins. My 2030 projection specifically assumes a 48% market share and 19% margins, putting PYPL at a $5.82 EPS and an 11.9x earnings multiple. I am more than comfortable with this price, which once again serves to underline my bullish conviction.

Cost Of Debt - Solid Cash Generation Reduces Need For Debt Financing

In a NOPLAT and Cost of Debt sensitivity table its visible that PYPL shares are undervalued at the moment. The lowest figures at 5% growth and 3,5% cost of debt giving it a share price of $72. On the high end of growth and current Cost of Debt the price is $80.37 per share. The cost of debt is based on the TTM interest expenses ($420 million) and total debt outstanding ($11.2 billion). Conclusion: the table supports a bullish thesis for PYPL showing upside in every cell.

Multiple Valuation

In my multiple analysis I include the 5.8 EPS I've mentioned earlier. The table shows that number in relation to different earnings multiples and a what if case that margins go beyond my assumption. The range I'm comfortable buying a fintech company like PYPL in is between 14 - 18 which is a price range of $81 - $105. At both ends its above current trading value. Once again this module supports my thesis.

Concluding the valuation segment I want to include a price range I believe in. The first one about scalability provides me with a base line for EPS assumptions, $5.7 - $5.9. The second one has me at $80 based on minimum 10% NOPLAT growth and that the cost of debt remains the same at 3.7%. The last one is a range of $81 - $105. Depending on the market environment I can see it reaching the higher end here. But for the sake of giving a more precise range I will stick with $80 - $90 by 2029. This implies an upside of 15.1% - 29.5%.

The Full Picture

PayPal operates as a large-scale online fintech enterprise serving many millions of customers. It currently holds a majority share of the market and consistently pursues internal improvements regarding margins. I now find the investments being directed into Venmo to be completely logical. That is precisely where growth is occurring, and I believe it is where PayPal should concentrate the majority of its resources as well.

If you remain unconvinced that the company can achieve solid EPS growth annually, simply observe the scale of their buybacks. It is truly incredible. Common shares have decreased by 7.7% in just six months, which is absurd for a mature entity like PYPL. You could literally be looking at returns in the low-teens every year if they sustain this pace. Yet, some claim PYPL is a poor investment. The numbers speak for themselves; they are solid across the entire business. The stock clearly exudes value, currently trading down 21% year-to-date.

For an extended period, bears contended that PayPal should be valued similarly to traditional bank stocks. It is effectively valued that way now, trading at 12 times forward earnings. This represents a massive discount compared to JP Morgan (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS), which are considered the gold standards for bank valuations. I simply do not grasp the reasoning for why PayPal should be valued lower than these institutions when its growth trajectory is notably better. Until someone presents me with compelling evidence to justify this discrepancy, I will continue to suggest there is upside for PayPal still.

data.title: The Fed-Trump Feud: Jamie Dimon Warns Interfering With Fed Would Push Up Rates
data.description:
Jamie Dimon Warns Interfering With Fed Would Push Up Rates; Inflation Holds Steady at 2.7% By Vicky Ge Huang

JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon defended the Federal Reserve after it was subpoenaed by the Justice Department, saying that "anything that chips away" at the central bank's independence "is not a good idea." Meanwhile, U.S. inflation remained relatively contained at the close of 2025 despite turbulence from tariffs, giving the Fed some breathing room as it weighs the threat of price pressures against a cooling labor market.

Top News JPMorgan Chief Reiterates His Support of Powell

Jamie Dimon, speaking with reporters after JPMorgan Chase issued its fourth-quarter earnings results, said political interference with the Fed would cause inflation and interest rates to go up , contrary to President Trump's stated goal of getting rates to go lower. Dimon's remarks came after Fed Chair Jerome Powell disclosed over the weekend that he was being investigated by the Justice Department.

Pace of Inflation Held Steady in December

Consumer prices rose 2.7% in December

from a year earlier, the Labor Department said Tuesday. That was unchanged from November's pace and in line with the expectations of economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal.

Central Banks Around the World Market Underprices Geopolitical Uncertainties, ECB Official Says

Financial markets aren't registering

increased geopolitical uncertainties that have raised downside risks to economic growth, the European Central Bank's vice president said Wednesday.

China Imports to Help Lower U.K. Inflation to Target, BOE's Taylor Says

The U.K.'s annual rate of inflation is likely to fall

to the Bank of England's 2% target by the middle of this year, aided by cheap imports from China and allowing for further cuts in borrowing costs, rate-setter Alan Taylor said Wednesday.

New Zealand's Foreign Minister Reprimands RBNZ Head for Powell Support

New Zealand's Foreign Minister Winston Peters reprimanded the country's central bank governor for signaling her support for Jerome Powell. Reserve Bank of New Zealand Gov. Anna Breman signed a joint letter of support for Powell this week following news of an investigation into the Fed leader.

"The Reserve Bank of New Zealand is statutorily independent of Central Government on matters of monetary policy. However, the RBNZ has no role, nor should it involve itself, in U.S. domestic politics," Peters said in a social media post. "We remind the Governor to stay in her New Zealand lane and stick to domestic monetary policy." -James Glynn

Financial Regulation Former New York City Mayor Eric Adams's NYC Token Plummets After $1 Million Drawdown

The value of a cryptocurrency launched Monday by former New York City Mayor Eric Adams went into freefall

after one of its creators pulled about $2.5 million from the project hours after its debut, according to blockchain analysts.

6 p.m.: FRB Richmond President Thomas Barkin speaks at VBA/VA Chamber Financial Forecast event

Commentary Trump Wants to Run the Economy Hot. There's a Good Chance He'll Succeed.

Most years, presidents don't have much impact on the economy; it is just too big and complicated. This year won't be like most years. President Trump is taking unprecedented steps to run the economy hot, and there is an excellent chance he'll succeed, writes WSJ's Greg Ip .

Washington has three big levers that affect growth: fiscal policy (taxes and spending), monetary policy (interest rates), and credit policy (the ease of borrowing). Historically, they were not coordinated: Fiscal policy followed the congressional cycle, monetary policy was set by an independent Fed and credit policy reflected often random decisions by regulators. This year, all three are dialed toward stimulus, reflecting a single-minded focus by Trump and congressional Republicans on faster economic growth. They hope that will deliver victory in the November midterm elections.

In the process, they are compromising other goals: taming debt, Fed independence and long-term financial stability. The consequences of that come later.

Basis Points When President Trump returned to the White House last year, economists predicted new tariffs would stifle China's massive export machine. Instead, China's trade surplus reached a record in 2025

at $1.19 trillion. Exports jumped 5.5% last year from 2024 in dollar-denominated terms, compared with 5.9% growth the prior year, China's customs agency reported Wednesday. The Bank of Korea is expected to keep its base rate unchanged at 2.5% this week. All 29 analysts polled by WSJ forecast no rate change at Thursday's policy meeting. Regarding the BOK's future rate moves, views are divided, with some analysts dropping their earlier forecasts of a possible cut. (Dow Jones Newswires) Indonesia's central bank has reiterated its defense of the rupiah, coming as volatility in Asian currencies tests the region's policymakers. Bank Indonesia said it will keep intervening to stabilize the rupiah, which is off to a poor start in 2026, hovering near the record lows against the dollar reached last in April. (DJN) About Us

data.title: Case For Japanese Yen Recovery is Weakening — Market Talk
data.description: The case for the Japanese yen to recover from its undervaluation is weakening, ING analysts say in a note. Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi plans to hold a snap election next month. She could pivot more towards super-loose fiscal and monetary policy if she secures a majority for her ruling Liberal Democratic Party, denting the yen, the analysts say. "Given Japanese consumer price inflation should be subdued in the first quarter, this looks the most likely window for [the dollar to rise to] 160/162 yen." However, Japan could still intervene at 160 to prop up the yen, they say. The dollar trades down 0.5% at 158.48 yen after hitting a one-and-a-half year high of 159.45 overnight, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: France bans 10 British far-right, anti-migration activists from entering
data.description:
France's interior ministry said on Wednesday it has banned 10 British far-right activists from entering or staying in the country, after they carried out actions deemed to incite violence and seriously disturb public order on French territory.

The activists, identified as members of a group called "Raise the Colours" that was involved in a national flag-raising campaign, seek to find and destroy boats used to carry migrants and spread propaganda on France's northern coast calling on the British public to join the movement to stop migration, according to the French interior ministry.

"Our rule of law is non-negotiable, violent or hate-inciting actions have no place on our territory," French Interior Minister Laurent Nunez wrote on social media platform X on Wednesday.

The ministry said in a statement it had been informed of the group's activities in December last year and that it had referred the matter to the relevant authorities, as the actions were likely to cause "serious disturbances" to public order.

"Raise the Colours" describes itself as a grassroots movement that began in the central English city of Birmingham, when a small group started tying national flags to lampposts in a show of national pride. It says the effort has since spread across the UK.

The widespread display of the red-and-white St George's Cross for England and the Union Jack for Britain has prompted concern among some migrant communities as a reflection of rising anti‑immigration sentiment in the country, coinciding with a wave of protests outside hotels housing asylum seekers last year.

Neither the group nor the British Foreign Office immediately responded to Reuters requests for comment.

Immigration and the crossings of small boats carrying migrants from France have become a focal point for British voters and has helped propel Nigel Farage's right-wing, anti-immigration Reform UK party, into a commanding opinion poll lead.

Farage last year in London met the leader of French far-right National Rally (RN) party, Jordan Bardella, who has accused France of being too soft on immigration.

data.title: Corn Prices Hover Near Three-Month Lows
data.description:
Corn futures traded around $4.20 per bushel, staying close to a three-month low of $4.17 reached on January 13, as markets remained weighed down by oversupply concerns and sluggish demand.

The USDA reported that farmers and grain companies held record corn inventories as of December 1, following a record 17 billion-bushel harvest, larger than previously estimated and up 14% from 2024 levels.

Looking ahead, the USDA has raised its forecast for the US corn harvest in marketing year 2025/26 by 6.81 million tonnes to a record 432.34 million tonnes.

The figure exceeds the previous 2023 record by 40 million tonnes and last year’s output by 54 million tonnes, adding further pressure to prices.

While projected US corn consumption was also revised higher by 2.3 million tonnes to 334.5 million tonnes, driven by increased feed demand, analysts had not anticipated such a sharp rise, raising the likelihood that part of the additional supply will ultimately flow into ending stocks.

data.title: ZAWYA-PRESSR: Bodour Al Qasimi highlights heritage-led hospitality during visit to Al Rayaheen Retreat
data.description:
Sharjah:  Her Highness Sheikha Bodour bint Sultan Al Qasimi, Chairperson of the Sharjah Investment and Development Authority (Shurooq), conducted a review visit to Al Rayaheen Retreat in Khorfakkan as part of Shurooq’s ongoing oversight of its heritage hospitality portfolio and commitment to delivering refined, culturally rooted guest experiences across the emirate.

During the visit, Her Highness was briefed on a series of carefully considered refinements implemented across the retreat, focused on strengthening the flow of the guest journey, elevating service readiness, and ensuring consistent operational standards, while preserving the site’s architectural identity and heritage character.

The recent enhancements reviewed during Her Highness’s visit reflect Shurooq’s broader strategy to continually refine and enrich its destinations while remaining grounded in Sharjah’s cultural identity. The elevations were implemented with a focus on guest comfort, operational efficiency, and experiential depth, without compromising the historical character that defines Al Rayaheen Retreat.

A heritage sanctuary shaped by place

Located in Khorfakkan’s historic Al Sharq district, Al Rayaheen Retreat offers a refined interpretation of traditional Arabian hospitality through a collection of carefully restored heritage homes. The retreat comprises 19 houses, ranging from one- to four-bedroom configurations, each offering private access, spacious living areas, outdoor seating, and fully equipped kitchenettes, ensuring comfort and privacy within an authentic cultural setting. Surrounded by an outdoor garden planted with palm, henna, and basil trees, the retreat reflects the district’s historic identity, once known locally as Freej Al Rayhan, meaning “the Basil Neighbourhood.” Guests can also explore the nearby heritage-style souq, designed to echo the character of a hidden historic chamber within the city.

The retreat features a reception, landscaped outdoor areas, and dining venues offering international cuisine in both indoor and outdoor settings. A curated selection of activities, including beach kayaking, jet skiing, scuba diving, hiking, and boat tours around Khorfakkan, further enhances the guest experience, subject to availability and weather conditions.

Heritage comfort, supported by thoughtful facilities

Al Rayaheen Retreat forms part of the Sharjah Collection, Shurooq’s homegrown hospitality brand that brings together a portfolio of nature-led and heritage-inspired destinations across the emirate. Each property within the collection is developed with a clear emphasis on authenticity, sustainability, and meaningful engagement with place.

Through continued investment in heritage-based hospitality, Shurooq reinforces Sharjah’s position as a destination where cultural continuity, responsible tourism, and contemporary hospitality converge to offer distinctive and enriching experiences for visitors.

data.title: Bank of America's Profit Climbs on Higher Revenue — WSJ
data.description:
By Alexander Saeedy

Bank of America said its profit rose 12% in the fourth quarter of last year, boosted by stronger revenue from its consumer bank and its sales and trading arm.

The bank said it earned $7.6 billion, or 98 cents per share. Analysts expected earnings of 96 cents per share. Full-year profit was $30.51 billion, up 13% versus last year.

Revenue for the fourth quarter rose to $28.37 billion. Analysts polled by FactSet expected $27.76 billion.

Consumer health appeared steady, with delinquencies of over 90 days on credit cards down 1.27% versus 1.35% a year ago. Card spending rose 6% from last year.

"With consumers and businesses proving resilient, as well as the regulatory environment and tax and trade policies coming into sharper focus, we expect further economic growth in the year ahead," CEO Brian Moynihan said in a press release. "We are bullish on the U.S. economy in 2026."

The country's biggest banks are reporting their quarterly financial results this week, offering a snapshot of a U.S. economy that has been contending with President Trump's tariffs, persistent inflation and weakening job prospects.

On Tuesday, JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon said consumers were still spending and that businesses remained healthy, though he warned of risks from geopolitical conditions. The bank reported higher spending on credit and debit cards, and said the over 90-day delinquency rate on credit cards edged lower.

data.title: Global Employment Stable But Decent Jobs In Short Supply: Report
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Global unemployment remains stable, but progress toward decent work has stalled, according to a new report from the International Labor Organization, which warns that young people continue to struggle in a job market which risks being further undermined by Artificial Intelligence and trade policy uncertainty.

According to the data compiled for the latest Employment and Social Trends 2026 report, the global unemployment rate is projected to stay stable at around 4.9 per cent this year, equivalent to some 186 million people out of work.

The biggest area of growth is in poorer countries - a reflection of the ageing population in richer economies, where fewer people of working age are available to enter or remain in work.

Job growth is projected to be 0.5 per cent in upper middle-income countries as opposed to 3.1 per cent in low-income nations.

However, being employed is not necessarily the same as having a quality job or a decent wage: nearly 300 million workers are living in extreme poverty, earning less than $3 a day.

It is expected that around 2.1 billion people will be working in the informal sector this year, with limited access to social protection, rights at work, and job security.

The global job situation for young people in low-income countries is described in the report as 'daunting', with more than a quarter neither in education, employment or training.

Educated youth in high-income countries are not immune from uncertainty. The study warns that AI and automation could make it harder for them to find work and calls for 'close monitoring' of the technology.

In 2025, the global economy was marked by the upheaval in international trade rules and tariff rates, led by the United States.

Trade supports around 465 million workers worldwide, more than half of them in Asia and the Pacific, and the uncertainty is cutting into workers' wages, especially in Southeast Asia, Southern Asia, and Europe, the report says.

Regarding North America, employment growth is slowing and job vacancies are declining, according to the report.

Unemployment rates are expected to increase in the medium term in both Canada and the United States, it said.
